title: Wild solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations whose singular sets in time have Hausdorff dimension strictly less than 1

def:1.1.summary: Weak solution of NSE on $T^3$: v $\in C^0([0,T)$;$L^2)$ that is weakly divergence-free at all t, has zero mean, and satisfies the distributional equation $\intv$₀$\cdot\varphi(\cdot,0) + \int\intv\cdot(\partial_t \varphi + (v\cdot\nabla)\varphi + \Delta\varphi) = 0$ for all div-free test functions $\varphi$.

thm:1.1.summary: Wild solution gluing theorem: there exists $\beta>0$ such that for any two strong solutions $u^1, u^2 \in C^0([0,T]$;Ḣ^3), there exists a weak solution v with v ≡ $u^1$ on [0,T/3] and v ≡ $u^2$ on [2T/3,T], with additional regularity v $\in C^0([0,T]$;$H^\beta\capW^{1,1+\beta})$, and the singular time set $\Sigma_T \subset (0,T]$ has Hausdorff dimension $< 1-\beta$. In particular, v is a.e. smooth.

rem:1.2.summary: Non-uniqueness of weak solutions for any strong initial datum: by taking $u^1=0$ and $u^2=shear$ flow, thm:1.1 gives non-unique weak solutions for any v₀$\in$Ḣ^3 (more generally for v₀ in any locally-well-posed class including $H^{1/2}, L^3$, BMO⁻^1).

rem:1.3.summary: First example of a mild/weak NSE solution with nonempty singular time set of Hausdorff dim STRICTLY LESS than 1. Improves on Buckmaster-Vicol (BV2019) where $dim(\Sigma_T) = 1$. Open problem: achieve dim < 1/2.

rem:1.4.summary: Non-uniqueness for very weak solutions (only $C^0_weak L^2)$ with any $L^2$ initial datum: for any Leray solution u, pick a regular time t₀, apply thm:1.1 on [t₀,T] to glue to a shear flow with different $L^2$ norm. Non-uniqueness in the very-weak class for all $L^2$ data.

thm:1.5.summary: Hyperdissipative extension: for $\alpha\in[1,5/4)$ there exists $\beta=\beta(\alpha)>0$ such that thm:1.1 holds for the hyperdissipative NSE (with $(-\Delta)^\alpha$ dissipation instead of $-\Delta)$. The convex integration scheme adapts to the more general dissipation.

prop:2.1.summary: Main Iteration Proposition: for a $= a(\alpha,b,\beta,\varepsilon_R)$ sufficiently large, one convex integration step takes $(v_q, R_q)$ satisfying NSE-Reynolds with inductive estimates and good set $G^{(q)}$ to $(v_{q+1}, R_{q+1})$ satisfying the same estimates at level q+1, with $||v_{q+1}-v_q||_{L^2} \le \delta_{q+1}^{1/2}$. Iteration of this step produces the wild solution.

prop:2.2.summary: Gluing construction: builds a mollified pair (v̄_q, R̄_q) solving NSE-Reynolds that agrees with $v_q$ on $G^{(q)}$, satisfies $L^2, H^3$ and derivative estimates (2.19), and has R̄_q = 0 for t near $G^{(q+1)}$. This enables the iteration to track the target solutions $u^1, u^2$ on the good set.

prop:3.1.summary: Local well-posedness for NSE: for v₀$\inH^3$, there exists a unique strong solution on [t₀,t₁] when t₁-t₀ $\le$ c/||v₀$||_{H^3}$, with energy bound (3.2a), $H^3$ bound (3.2b), and high-regularity decay estimate (3.4): |t-t₀$|^{N/(2\alpha)+M} ||\partial$ₜᴹ Dᴺ$v||_{H^3}$ ≲ ||v₀$||_{H^3}$.

prop:3.2.summary: Comparison estimate: for strong solution v of NSE on [t₀,t₁] and Reynolds solution $v_q$ with $R_q$, the difference satisfies $||v-v_q||_{L^p} \le$ C|t-t₀$| ||\nablaR_q||_{L^p}$ and $||R(v-v_q)||_{L^p} \le$ C|t-t₀$| ||R_q||_{L^p}$. Crucial for showing v̄_q approximates $v_q$ well on good intervals.

cor:3.3.summary: Explicit comparison bounds: $||v-v_q||_{L^\inftyL^2} \le |t$₁-t₀$|\lambda_q^5$ and $||R(v-v_q)||_{L^\inftyL^1} \le |t$₁-t₀$|\lambda_q^{-3\varepsilon_R/4}\delta_{q+1}$. Used in prop:2.2 to control the gluing error.

lem:4.1.summary: Beltrami wave geometry: for $\alpha=1,2$ there exist sets $\Lambda_\alpha \subset S^2\capQ^3$ and smooth functions $\gamma_\xi$: $N\to\mathbb{R}$ such that any symmetric matrix R with $|R-Id|\le1/2$ decomposes as R $= \Sigma_{\xi\in\Lambda_\alpha} \gamma^2_\xi(R)(\xi$⊗$\xi)$. This is the key geometric lemma enabling the NSE-Reynolds error to be cancelled by Beltrami waves.

lem:4.2.summary: Temporal cutoff bounds: for $q\ge0$ there exists $i_max(q)$ such that $\chi_{(i)} = 0$ for i $> i_max$, with $\rho_i \le \tau_{q+1}^{-2}, \Sigma\rho_i^{1/2}2^{-i} \le \lambda_q^{-\varepsilon_R/16}\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}$, and $||\chi_{(i)}||_{L^2}$ ≲ 2^{-i}, $||\chi_{(i)}||_{C^N_{x,t}}$ ≲ $\tau_{q+1}^{-3N}$. Controls the temporal intermittency.

lem:4.3.summary: Amplitude function bounds: $||a_{(\xi)}||_{L^2}$ ≲ $\rho_i^{1/2}2^{-i}$ ≲ $\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}, ||a_{(\xi)}||_{L^\infty}$ ≲ $\rho_i^{1/2}$ ≲ $\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}2^i, ||a_{(\xi)}||_{C^N_{x,t}}$ ≲ $\tau_{q+1}^{-3N-1}$. The amplitude functions encode the Reynolds stress decomposition.

prop:4.4.summary: Velocity perturbation component bounds: principal part $||w^{(p)}_{q+1}||_{L^2} \le \delta_{q+1}^{1/2}/2$ and $W^{N,p}$ ≲ $\tau^{-2}$ℓ_⊥^{2/p-1}ℓ_‖$^{1/p-1/2}\lambda_{q+1}^N$; incompressibility and temporal correctors are $O(\mu$⁻^1) smaller. Verifies that the Beltrami wave perturbation has the right size.

lem:4.5.summary: Fast oscillation / Riemann-Lebesgue lemma: for rapidly $(1/\kappa)$ oscillating g and slowly varying f with $||D^j f||_{L^p} \le C_f \lambda^j (with \lambda \ll \kappa)$, we have $||fg||_{L^p}$ ≲ $C_f ||g||_{L^p}$. Enables extraction of leading-order contribution from the product of the amplitude and the Beltrami wave.

prop:4.6.summary: Total velocity perturbation bounds: $||w_{q+1}||_{L^2} \le 3\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}/4, ||v_{q+1}-v_q||_{L^2} \le \delta_{q+1}^{1/2}, ||w_{q+1}||_{W^{s,p}}$ ≲ $\tau^{-2}$ℓ_⊥^{2/p-1}ℓ_‖$^{1/p-1/2}\lambda_{q+1}^s$, and $||\partial_t w_{q+1}||_{H^2}$ ≲ $\lambda_{q+1}^5$. Complete $W^{s,p}$ control of the full perturbation.

prop:5.1.summary: New Reynolds stress closing: there exist R̃ and pressure p̃ such that $v_{q+1}$ satisfies $\partial_t v_{q+1} + div(v_{q+1}$⊗$v_{q+1}) + \nablap$̃ $+ (-\Delta)^\alpha v_{q+1} =$ div R̃, with ||R̃$||_{L^p}$ ≲ $\lambda_{q+1}^{-2\varepsilon_R}\delta_{q+2}$ and supp R̃ $\subset {dist(t,G^{(q+1)}) > \tau_{q+1}}$. The new error R̃ is STRICTLY SMALLER than $R_q$.

cor:5.2.summary: Main iterative step completion: there exists $(v_{q+1}, p_{q+1}, R_{q+1})$ solving NSE-Reynolds at level q+1, with $R_{q+1}(t) = 0$ on $G^{(q+1)}, ||R_{q+1}||_{L^1} \le \lambda_{q+1}^{-\varepsilon_R}\delta_{q+2} (5.4a)$, and $||R_{q+1}||_{H^3} \le \lambda_{q+1}^7 (5.4b)$. This is the inductive hypothesis at level q+1.

edges.note: thm:1.1 follows from iterating prop:2.1 (main iteration) finitely many times; the iteration converges in $L^2$ by the $||v_{q+1}-v_q||_{L^2} \le \delta_{q+1}^{1/2}$ bound with $\Sigma\delta_{q+1}^{1/2} < \infty$
thm:1.1 uses prop:2.2 for the gluing initialization: builds (v̄_0, R̄_0) that matches $u^1$ on $G^{(0)}$ and launches the iteration. Without gluing, the iteration cannot track $u^1$ and $u^2$.
thm:1.5 is a direct extension of thm:1.1 to hyperdissipative NSE $\alpha\in[1,5/4)$; the proof scheme is identical with the same estimates adapted to $(-\Delta)^\alpha$
prop:2.1 one iteration step is proved by applying prop:5.1 (R̃ bound) and cor:5.2 (construct $(v_{q+1},R_{q+1})$ satisfying NSE-Reynolds at level q+1 with all inductive estimates)
prop:2.1 uses prop:4.6 to verify the velocity inductive estimates $||v_{q+1}-v_q||_{L^2} \le \delta_{q+1}^{1/2}$ and the $W^{s,p}$ bounds on $w_{q+1}$
prop:2.1 uses prop:3.2 (and cor:3.3) to establish that the glued piece v̄_q approximates $v_q$ well on intervals in $G^{(q+1)}$
prop:2.2 gluing construction uses prop:3.1 (local well-posedness) to define the strong solution pieces that interpolate between the target solutions $u^1,u^2$ on the good time intervals
prop:2.2 uses prop:3.2 to control ||v̄_q $- v_q||_{L^2} (eq$:2.19.c) by bounding the departure from strong solution behavior over the short intervals between $G^{(q)}$ and $G^{(q+1)}$
cor:5.2 derives the $L^1$ and $H^3$ bounds on $R_{q+1}$ directly from the $L^p$ bound ||R̃$||_{L^p}$ ≲ $\lambda_{q+1}^{-2\varepsilon_R}\delta_{q+2}$ from prop:5.1
prop:5.1 (Reynolds stress closing) uses the $W^{s,p}$ bounds on $w_{q+1}$ from prop:4.6 to bound the oscillation error and transport error terms in R̃
prop:5.1 uses the support property of R̄_q from prop:2.2 (R̄_q = 0 on $G^{(q+1)})$ to establish supp R̃ $\subset {dist(t,G^{(q+1)}) > \tau_{q+1}}$
prop:4.6 total perturbation bounds use prop:4.4 (individual bounds on $w^{(p)}, w^{(c)}, w^{(t)})$ as inputs for the triangle inequality estimates
prop:4.6 uses amplitude bounds from lem:4.3 in the estimates for $||w_{q+1}||_{L^2}$ and $||\partial_t w_{q+1}||_{H^2}$

prop:4.4 construction of the Beltrami wave perturbation $w^{(p)}$ uses lem:4.1 to decompose the Reynolds stress R̄_q as a sum of rank-1 terms $\gamma^2_\xi(R$̄$_q)(\xi$⊗$\xi)$, generating the individual Beltrami waves $W_{(\xi)}$
prop:4.4 uses the temporal cutoff bounds lem:$4.2 (i_max$ finiteness, $\rho_i$ bounds, $\chi_{(i)}$ norms) for the $L^2, C^N$ estimates on the principal part $w^{(p)}$
prop:4.4 uses amplitude function bounds lem:4.3 for the $L^2, L^\infty, C^N$ estimates on $a_{(\xi)}$

prop:4.4 uses lem:4.5 (fast oscillation lemma) to extract the leading order $|w^{(p)}|^2$ ~ $\Sigma\gamma^2_\xi(R$̄$_q)|W_{(\xi)}|^2$ from the $a_{(\xi)} * B_{(\xi)}$ product, cancelling the Reynolds stress
cor:3.3 is an immediate consequence of prop:3.2 with specific parameter choices (a large, t₁ satisfying prop:3.2's condition)
prop:3.2 uses prop:3.1 to guarantee existence of the unique strong solution v on [t₀,t₁], from which the comparison estimate is derived
rem:1.2 (non-uniqueness) is derived by specializing thm:1.1 with $u^1=0, u^2=shear$ flow
rem:1.3 (Hausdorff dim < 1) contextualizes the dim $< 1-\beta$ result from thm:1.1
rem:1.4 (very weak $L^2$ non-uniqueness) extends rem:1.2 to $L^2$ data using Leray solutions and thm:1.1

main_results.description: Definition of weak solutions, wild solution gluing theorem, and corollaries

convex_integration_scheme.description: Main iteration proposition and gluing construction — the top-level inductive framework

local_wellposedness_and_comparison.description: Local $H^3$ strong solutions and comparison with Reynolds solutions for the gluing argument

intermittent_beltrami_perturbation.description: Intermittent Beltrami wave velocity perturbation: geometry, amplitudes, cutoffs, and full bounds

reynolds_stress_closing.description: Verify that the new Reynolds stress $R_{q+1}$ satisfies the inductive estimates at level q+1

BV.title: Nonuniqueness of weak solutions to the Navier-Stokes equation
BV.note: Direct predecessor: BV2019 proves non-uniqueness of weak NSE solutions with singular time set $dim(\Sigma_T) = 1$; BCV2021 (this paper) improves to dim < 1 while also gluing between strong solutions. Co-author Vicol.

Isett16.title: A proof of Onsager's conjecture
Isett16.note: Isett's proof of Onsager's conjecture; the convex integration scheme in this paper (BCV) builds on techniques developed for the Onsager program

BDLSV17.title: Onsager's conjecture for admissible weak solutions
BDLSV17.note: Cited as background for convex integration techniques for Euler/NSE
